title: Contract Request: Interfolio (University of Massachusetts, Amherst)

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Massachusetts Public Records Law, I hereby request the following records:

The most recent contract, governing, base, or master license agreement with Elsevier relating to the licensing of Interfolio;
All related terms and conditions, terms of service, amendments, addenda, appendices, attachments, product terms, MOUs, MOAs, data processing agreements, and/or letters of intent; 
All financial documents or records of financial transactions, exchanges or obligations related to the contractual relations with Elsevier for faculty information system technology;
and
Assessment documentation including, but not limited to, the Higher Education Community Vendor Assessment Toolkit (HECVAT). 

Responsive materials include all contracts, agreements, amendments, appendices, invoices, and any other documents listed above that incorporate language governing terms of use of Interfolio.

The scope of this request should encompass more than just direct contracts or renewals between the university and Elsevier; please incorporate all applicable agreements and supporting records facilitated through consortium entities (such as Lyrasis).

I also request that, if appropriate, fees be waived as we believe this request is in the public interest, as suggested but not stipulated by the recommendations of the Massachusetts Supervisor of Public Records. The requested documents will be made available to the general public free of charge as part of the public information service at MuckRock.com, and is not made for commercial usage.

I expect the request to be filled in an accessible format, including for screen readers, which provide text-to-speech for persons unable to read print. Files that are not accessible to screen readers include, for example, .pdf image files as well as physical documents.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Michele Gibney

title: This submission is a formal follow-up/addendum regarding open Public Records Request #R007027-040726 and FOIA Appeal 2026-209

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the DC Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

VIA EMAIL & MUCKROCK PUBLIC RECORD (Req #212771)

To: Mayor's Office of Legal Counsel (MOLC)
Cc: M. Claudine Sassa, Senior Paralegal & FOIA Officer, DISB <claudine.sassa@dc.gov>
Cc: Lilah Blackstone, DISB Acting General Counsel <lilah.blackstone@dc.gov>
Cc: Karima M. Woods, Commissioner, DISB <karima.woods@dc.gov>
Cc: Daniel Lucas, Inspector General for the District of Columbia <daniel.lucas@dc.gov>
Cc: DC Office of the Inspector General Intake <oig@dc.gov>
Cc: Brian Schwalb, Attorney General for the District of Columbia <brian.schwalb@dc.gov>
Cc: Kenyan McDuffie, DC Councilmember <kenyan.mcduffie@dc.gov>
Cc: DC Consumer Protection <consumer.protection@dc.gov>

Subject: REBUTTAL TO DISB SUPPLEMENTAL RESPONSE – FOIA Appeal 2026-209 (Public Records #R007027-040726)

Dear Mayor's Office of Legal Counsel,

I am in receipt of the Department of Insurance, Securities and Banking’s (DISB) supplemental response dated June 5, 2026, regarding FOIA Appeal 2026-209. This appeal is now being actively tracked on the public transparency platform MuckRock. 

DISB’s continued reliance on D.C. Official Code § 2-534(a)(1) ("Exemption 1") to execute a blanket withholding of 92 pages is not only a gross misapplication of the statute, but it raises a peculiar and deeply troubling question: Why is a District regulatory agency fighting so aggressively to hide standard accounting files? 

It defies logic that DISB is expending vast administrative resources to shield standard legal invoices and line-item billing summaries generated by Housing Counseling Services—an entity utilized by DISB's own Foreclosure Ombudsman—under the absurd guise of "corporate trade secrets." This intense administrative stonewalling suggests an effort to conceal the transactional trail of how this 203(k) predatory lending scheme was internally managed and ultimately buried by regulatory officials.

Furthermore, DISB's June 5 letter fails entirely to satisfy the MOLC's remand order. On June 1, 2026, the MOLC explicitly instructed DISB to adequately explain how the withheld records fall within the scope of the cited exemption, noting that DISB had "not sufficiently explained" this connection. 

Under established FOIA precedent, an agency cannot rely on a single, boilerplate paragraph to unilaterally hide 92 pages of documents. To justify such withholdings, an agency is required to produce a line-by-line explanation (a Vaughn Index) detailing precisely why specific text on specific pages meets the statutory definition of a trade secret. DISB has completely ignored this requirement, offering only a sweeping, generalized refusal.

Standard billing rates and service descriptions for foreclosure mediation are administrative records of public concern. They are not secret formulas or commercially valuable intellectual property.

I respectfully request that the MOLC order the immediate release of the 92 pages. If DISB insists on continuing this charade, they must be compelled to produce a legally compliant, line-by-line Vaughn Index justifying every single redaction, rather than being permitted to execute a blanket concealment of these public records.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 15 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

FRED GAMBLE JR
